title: Unconventional PR
summary: Unconventional PR is the traction channel where you create news rather than pitch it — publicity stunts, viral videos, contests, and systematized customer delight that force press and word-of-mouth to come to you. This chapter covers the attention-arbitrage mechanism that makes stunts work, the business profiles where the channel fits (and the ones where it torches budget), the specific tactics, how to test them cheaply, and the failure modes — brand damage, one-hit-wonder decay, and misread audiences.

concepts.title: Unconventional PR: Creating News Instead of Pitching It

pretest.prompt: A founder emails 200 journalists a well-crafted press release about a new feature and gets three replies, none of which become stories. A competitor dresses their mascot in a bull costume, runs around a conference getting kicked out of the convention center, and ends up in TechCrunch the next morning. Before reading on — what structural difference between those two approaches explains the asymmetric result, and what does that tell you about what 'unconventional PR' actually is?
pretest.modelAnswer: The press-release founder is *pitching news*: asking a reporter to agree that his feature is interesting enough to write about, competing against hundreds of other pitches for the same slot. The bull-costume founder is *creating news*: producing an event that is itself a story, so reporters cover it the way they'd cover any spectacle — the decision has already been made for them. Unconventional PR is the traction channel where you engineer events, artifacts, or gestures that are independently newsworthy, so coverage happens as a byproduct of the event existing rather than as a favor you have to beg for. The asymmetry isn't about craft — it's about which side of the pitch/create line you're on.

lesson.markdown:
## The definition

Unconventional PR is the traction channel in which you **generate press and word-of-mouth by creating something inherently newsworthy, rather than by persuading a reporter that your existing news is interesting**. Conventional PR (the sister channel in Chapter 7) takes the product as given and works the media relationship — pitches, press releases, exclusives, embargoes. Unconventional PR takes the media relationship as hard-to-move and *changes the product of the news instead*. The output in both cases can be a New York Times write-up; the mechanism for getting there is almost inverted.

The load-bearing word is *create*. A stunt, a viral video, a contest with a surprising prize, a 600-pound block of ice in front of your competitor's conference — each is a fact in the world before it is a pitch. Reporters don't have to take your word that it's interesting; they can see it is. That removes the largest friction in conventional PR (reporter skepticism) and replaces it with a different problem (coming up with something that is actually interesting), which is a problem you can solve alone, on your own schedule, without a Rolodex.

## Two shapes

The Traction authors split the channel cleanly in two:

1. **Publicity stunts** — one-shot engineered spectacles designed to generate a burst of media coverage. Half.com paying a small Oregon town to rename itself Half.com. DuckDuckGo buying a billboard in Google's backyard about privacy. WePay dropping a block of ice at PayPal's developer conference labeled 'PayPal freezes your accounts.' These are high-variance, high-ceiling, and episodic.
2. **Customer appreciation** — small, repeatable, scalable gestures that turn individual customers into evangelists. Hipmunk mailing handwritten notes and luggage tags. Grasshopper sending cookies and Starbucks gift cards. Zappos treating support calls as marketing rather than cost. These are low-variance, lower-ceiling per event, but *compound* because every customer is a potential channel.

The two shapes are not alternatives — well-run unconventional-PR programs use both. Stunts deliver spikes; appreciation delivers a baseline of positive word-of-mouth that keeps producing while you plan the next stunt.

## Why this counts as a distinct channel

You might object that stunts are just a creative tactic inside PR. The reason Weinberg and Mares call it a separate channel: the *skill set*, the *cost structure*, and the *decision-makers* differ. Conventional PR rewards relationships and crisp pitches. Unconventional PR rewards a different muscle — noticing what would be genuinely surprising in your market, taking the reputational risk to execute it, and having an internal team that can build a block of ice, film a launch video, or hire a stuntman in a bull costume. David Hauser hired two full-time employees at Grasshopper specifically to run unconventional PR; you don't staff a 'stunts team' unless you've accepted this is its own discipline.

## The crowding argument

There's a second reason this is worth treating as its own channel. Conventional PR is crowded — every company pitches. Unconventional PR is far less crowded because stunts are scarier, harder to justify to a board, and require creative taste most teams don't think of themselves as having. That scarcity is the arbitrage: fewer competitors for a scarce resource (journalist attention), higher returns per dollar spent when you execute well.

workedExample.setup: Priya runs a 6-person consumer app called Thrifter that finds secondhand furniture nearby. She has $20k of marketing budget and wants a press moment at launch. Her head of marketing proposes the standard playbook: a polished press release, 30 targeted pitches to tech reporters, a Product Hunt launch, and a paid promo budget behind the coverage. Priya is asking whether she should instead be thinking about unconventional PR.
workedExample.walkthrough:
Apply the create-vs-pitch distinction to each plan.

**The press-release plan is pitching.** The news is 'we launched an app.' Every reporter has seen ten of those this week. Thrifter's press release is competing with thirty other identical pitches for the same dozen slots. Best case: 2–3 small write-ups, most of which never drive meaningful signups.

**What a create move would look like.** Priya's team brainstorms: what could they do that would be independently newsworthy and consistent with Thrifter's brand? Candidates: (a) rent a U-Haul and drive around a single Brooklyn neighborhood for a weekend giving away every piece of furniture on their own platform, covering the 'last-mile' for free; (b) set up a pop-up 'free furniture museum' in an NYC storefront where the entire inventory is Thrifter listings and everything is free if you pick it up; (c) commission an oversized, absurd piece of furniture (a 20-foot couch) and park it outside IKEA.

Each of these is a *fact* — once it exists, reporters cover it because it's a story, not because Priya convinced them it is. (a) also creates organic social content (videos of people receiving free furniture). (b) creates a physical place reporters can visit. (c) creates visuals every local news station will run.

**The budget flips.** The press release plan spent $5k on a PR agency plus $5k on a Product Hunt promo. The stunt plan spends $3k on the U-Haul weekend, $1k on giveaway inventory Thrifter is covering from existing listings, and $2k on a photographer/videographer to capture assets. Remaining budget goes to amplification *after* the stunt works, not to begging for the first piece of coverage.

**The appreciation baseline.** Alongside the stunt, Priya assigns one person to mail a handwritten thank-you card plus a $10 Starbucks card to the first 500 users who complete a pickup. This is the customer-appreciation side of the same channel — it won't generate a spike, but it will seed the kind of tweets and word-of-mouth that reporters notice when deciding which app to cover next.

Priya's conclusion: swap the default pitch-driven plan for a create-driven one, reserving conventional PR as the amplifier that kicks in *after* the stunt has produced something reporters want to write about anyway.

contrast.explanation: Viral marketing and unconventional PR both produce free exposure, but the mechanism is different. Viral marketing is a *structural property of the product*: users generate users through use. Unconventional PR is an *event or gesture you produce* to earn press and word-of-mouth. A viral loop doesn't need a reporter or a journalist's attention; a publicity stunt does. Many companies confuse the two because a viral video (Dollar Shave Club, Will It Blend?) can live in either camp — the test is whether the next user comes from *using the product* (viral) or from *watching content about the product* (unconventional PR). Dollar Shave Club's launch video is unconventional PR that fed paid and organic signup flows, not a product-level viral loop.

flashcards.front: What is the one-sentence definition of unconventional PR as a traction channel?
flashcards.back: The channel in which you generate press and word-of-mouth by creating something inherently newsworthy — stunts, spectacles, gestures — rather than by pitching reporters on news you already have.

flashcards.front: Conventional PR ______ news; unconventional PR ______ news.
flashcards.back: pitches ; creates — conventional PR asks a reporter to cover a story you already have; unconventional PR engineers an event that is itself a story.

flashcards.front: Name the two shapes of unconventional PR the chapter distinguishes, and give the defining trait of each.
flashcards.back: (1) Publicity stunts — one-shot engineered spectacles designed to spike media coverage; high-variance, episodic. (2) Customer appreciation — small, scalable, repeatable gestures that turn customers into evangelists; low-variance, compounding.

flashcards.front: Which of these is unconventional PR and which is conventional PR? (a) Sending a carefully targeted pitch to a TechCrunch reporter about a product milestone. (b) Mailing chocolate-covered grasshoppers to 5,000 influential people with a video about entrepreneurship.
flashcards.back: (a) is conventional PR — persuading a reporter to cover existing news. (b) is unconventional PR (Grasshopper's 5000 campaign) — creating a physical, surprising artifact that is itself the story reporters then cover.

flashcards.front: Why do Weinberg and Mares treat unconventional PR as a separate channel rather than a tactic inside conventional PR?
flashcards.back: Because the skill set (creative stunt design vs. relationship pitching), the cost structure (production-led vs. outreach-led), and the in-house team required (stunts can justify full-time hires — Grasshopper had two) are distinct. The crowding is also different: conventional PR is saturated; unconventional PR is relatively uncrowded, which is where the return comes from.

scenarios.setup: A seed-stage founder says: 'We just did unconventional PR — we sent out a really well-crafted press release with a punchy headline and got picked up by three blogs.'
scenarios.question: Is this unconventional PR? Why or why not?
scenarios.modelAnswer: No — this is conventional PR with good copywriting. A 'punchy' press release is still a pitch: you are asking a reporter to agree that your existing news is worth covering, and the reporter is deciding whether to oblige. Unconventional PR requires you to create an event, artifact, or gesture that is independently newsworthy before the reporter encounters it. The test: if every reporter in the world ignored you, would the thing you did still exist as a story people talk about? A press release fails that test (nothing exists without a reporter's agreement). A billboard in Google's backyard, a block of ice at a competitor's conference, or a town renamed Half.com passes it. The founder is mislabeling the channel, which matters because the failure modes and the right next moves are different for each.
rubric: Identifies the activity as conventional PR, not unconventional
Names the create-vs-pitch distinction
Applies a clean test (does the thing exist as a story without the reporter?)
Explains why mislabeling the channel matters (different failure modes, different next moves)

scenarios.setup: A founder of a prosumer video editing tool has $15k and six weeks before a competitor's product launch. She wants a press moment that lands during her competitor's launch week, partly to steal oxygen.
scenarios.question: Design a create-driven move (not a pitch-driven one) that fits this budget and timing. Name at least one concrete idea, and explain why it creates news rather than pitching news.
scenarios.modelAnswer: A defensible create move: on the morning of the competitor's launch, publish a fully produced side-by-side 'speedrun' video in which the founder edits an identical 60-second clip in her tool and in the competitor's tool simultaneously, on a split screen, showing final times and final quality. Post raw assets (project files, footage) so anyone can replicate. Invite a well-known editor to narrate. Budget: $6k for the editor, $3k for the video production, $3k for a micro-influencer seeding push, $3k reserve. Why it's create-driven: the video is an object that exists as a story regardless of whether any reporter picks it up — YouTube, Twitter, and editing communities will circulate it on its own. Reporters who do cover it are covering something they can watch, not something they have to take on faith. It also hijacks the competitor's news day without pitching against them directly. Alternative ideas: a 'one-cut challenge' contest during launch week with a cash prize for the best edit done in her tool; a storefront 'edit bay' in the competitor's city where any passerby can get a reel edited for free. Both share the same property: the event exists first; press coverage follows.
rubric: Proposes a concrete, executable create-move within budget
Names why the idea exists as a story without a reporter's blessing
Shows the timing/steal-oxygen logic of landing during the competitor's launch
Keeps the plan inside the $15k budget with a specific breakdown
Avoids collapsing back into a pitch-driven plan (press release + outreach)

teachback.prompt: In 60 seconds, teach a first-time founder what unconventional PR is. Cover (1) the one-sentence definition, (2) the create-vs-pitch distinction and why that distinction matters, (3) the two shapes (stunts and customer appreciation), and (4) why this is treated as a separate channel rather than a tactic inside conventional PR.
rubric: States the definition precisely (creating newsworthy events, not pitching existing news)
Names the create-vs-pitch distinction and gives at least one consequence of it
Distinguishes the two shapes with one example each
Explains the crowding / skill-set argument for treating it as a separate channel
Explanation is causal, not a list — the 'why' of each part is clear

pretest.prompt: Half.com spent $70k to rename a town and got 40 million impressions. A competitor spent $70k on a well-run PR agency pitching the same launch and got three small write-ups. Before reading on — what general mechanism explains the 100x-plus difference in output per dollar, and under what conditions would you expect the gap to narrow or flip?
pretest.modelAnswer: The mechanism is attention arbitrage: journalists' attention is a scarce resource, and the 'normal pitch' market for it is extremely crowded (hundreds of pitches per reporter per week), so the marginal return on another well-crafted pitch is low. A genuinely unusual event faces almost no competition — there is exactly one company renaming a town this year — so it sits in a much less crowded market for the same scarce resource, yielding a much higher conversion of dollars to attention. The gap narrows or flips when: (a) too many companies copy the same stunt format (crowding in the unconventional market), (b) the stunt is unusual but not *relevant* to the reporter's beat or audience (no conversion), or (c) the founder's audience ignores press entirely (e.g., deep enterprise B2B buyers who buy from peer referrals, not news).

lesson.markdown:
## What's actually being traded

The mechanism underneath unconventional PR is an arbitrage on **journalist attention** — and, downstream, on reader attention. Both are scarce. Reporters at major outlets receive hundreds of pitches a week, almost all structured the same way: 'Here is our news, here is a quote, here is why your readers care.' A well-crafted pitch is one of many identical offers for the same slot, which is why conventional PR is high-effort for modest yield.

A stunt does not compete in that market. When Half.com renamed Halfway, Oregon, no other startup was offering a town-rename story that day. When WePay dropped a block of ice at PayPal's conference, the reporter wasn't choosing between WePay's ice and ten other ice blocks. The stunt sits in a nearly empty market for the same attention — a classic arbitrage condition: abundant buyers (reporters and readers who want unusual stories), scarce sellers (companies willing to do something genuinely unusual).

## Why the arbitrage persists

You might expect the gap to close as founders notice it. It doesn't, for structural reasons:

1. **Stunts feel risky to approve.** A board that greenlights a $20k PR retainer will balk at a $20k block-of-ice idea even when the expected value is higher, because the stunt has a larger downside if it flops (looking silly in public) and no template to point to. The career incentives inside most companies push toward conventional options.
2. **Stunts require creative taste.** Coming up with something that is surprising *and* on-brand *and* legal *and* appropriate for your audience is a real skill. Most marketing teams don't feel like they have it, so they default to what they can execute confidently.
3. **Stunts are embarrassing when they fail publicly.** An unread press release is invisible; a failed stunt is a story of its own kind. The asymmetry of embarrassment keeps many founders out of the market even when the math favors entry.

These frictions are *why the arbitrage exists at all*. If stunts were as safe and systematizable as press releases, every company would do both, and the returns per dollar would equalize.

## The conversion half of the mechanism

Coverage is necessary but not sufficient. The second half of the mechanism is whether coverage converts to signups, customers, or brand recall. Half.com's 40 million impressions produced a large customer base partly because the story naturally communicated *what Half.com was* (a cheaper alternative, hence 'half'). DuckDuckGo's Google-backyard billboard converted because the stunt's payload was the *positioning itself* — privacy — and reporters who covered it couldn't help explaining DuckDuckGo's differentiator in the same breath.

The best stunts carry the message inside the spectacle. A stunt that gets attention without communicating what the product is produces impressions without lift — the one-hit-wonder trap covered later in the chapter.

## The ceiling

Arbitrages close when everyone notices them. The reason unconventional PR remains durable is that the frictions above are *structural*, not informational — founders know stunts work; they still don't do them. But within specific formats, the arbitrage does close. Everyone can now produce a launch video, so 'film a funny launch video' is no longer scarce. The durable play is always one level up: look for formats that are still unusual in your specific market, because the arbitrage lives in scarcity, and scarcity is local.

workedExample.setup: Marco runs a Series-A logistics-software company for trucking fleets. His competitor just raised $40M and got a big TechCrunch write-up. Marco wants to respond with something that punches above his budget. His CMO suggests spending $50k on a PR agency to pitch 'our CEO's response.' Marco is skeptical.
workedExample.walkthrough:
Walk through the arbitrage frame.

**The CMO's plan lives in the crowded market.** Reporters who just covered the competitor are not looking for 'rival CEO weighs in' follow-ups — that's one of twenty pitches they're getting this week. Marco's $50k buys relationships and crisp copy, but the underlying good is oversupplied. Expected outcome: one or two small write-ups, minimal lift.

**What does the empty market look like in trucking software?** A stunt needs to be surprising to *this* audience — dispatchers, fleet owners, owner-operators. The empty market here is not a 20-foot couch or a block of ice; those are surprising to tech reporters but not to truckers. The empty market is something like: Marco's team rents a truck, drives it cross-country with a live dashboard projected on the trailer showing real-time fuel savings using their software, stopping at truck stops to buy meals for drivers who sign up. Alternatively: a public challenge where Marco's company reimburses any trucking firm that can prove his competitor's software saved them more on a standardized route.

**Why this arbitrages.** Trucking-industry journalists get almost no stunts on their beat. A single execution would plausibly dominate trade publications for weeks. Marco's $50k goes much further in that empty market than in the saturated tech-reporter market.

**The stunt has to carry the message.** 'Fuel savings' is Marco's differentiator. The cross-country truck with the live dashboard *is* the message — reporters who cover it cannot avoid explaining the savings pitch. This is the payload-inside-the-spectacle principle; without it, even a successful stunt converts poorly.

**Why approval is hard.** Marco's board will ask why he's 'playing games' instead of running standard PR. The honest answer is the arbitrage: the standard market is crowded, the stunt market is nearly empty in trucking, and the expected value per dollar is several-fold higher even accounting for flop risk. That's a real argument, but not every board buys it, which is exactly why the arbitrage persists.

contrast.explanation: The create-vs-pitch distinction (prior concept) is the channel's *definition*. The attention-arbitrage mechanism is *why* that distinction produces disproportionate results per dollar. The definition tells you what unconventional PR is; the mechanism tells you why it's not redundant with conventional PR. A founder can know the definition and still underinvest if they don't see the arbitrage — they'll treat stunts as 'fun but unserious' rather than as the higher-expected-value option when the conventional market is crowded.

flashcards.front: What scarce resource does unconventional PR arbitrage, and what makes it an arbitrage?
flashcards.back: Journalist (and reader) attention. It's an arbitrage because the 'normal pitch' market for that attention is crowded while the 'unusual event' market is nearly empty — same scarce good, different competition levels, so dollars convert at very different rates.

flashcards.front: Name three structural reasons the attention arbitrage persists rather than closing.
flashcards.back: (1) Stunts are risky to approve inside companies (career incentives favor safer conventional PR). (2) Stunts require creative taste that most marketing teams don't believe they have. (3) Failed stunts are publicly embarrassing in a way unread press releases aren't, so the downside-asymmetry keeps entrants out.

flashcards.front: A stunt that grabs attention without communicating what the product actually is produces ______ without ______ — the one-hit-wonder trap.
flashcards.back: impressions ; lift — coverage doesn't convert because the spectacle didn't carry the message payload inside it.

flashcards.front: Why is 'film a funny launch video' no longer a reliable unconventional-PR play in 2026, even though Dollar Shave Club's 2012 launch video worked?
flashcards.back: Because the arbitrage closes as formats crowd. Funny launch videos are now common, so the 'empty market' in that format is gone. The durable move is always one level up — find a format still unusual in your specific market, because scarcity is local and arbitrages close when copied.

flashcards.front: What's the 'payload inside the spectacle' principle?
flashcards.back: The best stunts embed the product's actual message or differentiator inside the spectacle itself, so reporters who cover the stunt cannot avoid explaining the product. Half.com's rename told you it was half-priced; DuckDuckGo's Google-backyard billboard told you it was private. Without the payload, coverage produces impressions but not conversion.

scenarios.setup: A founder proposes: 'Let's do a big, weird stunt at CES — we'll have someone in a gorilla suit hand out flyers. It worked for Chargify's bull costume at SXSW.'
scenarios.question: Using the attention-arbitrage frame, diagnose whether this is likely to work. What specifically should the founder check before executing?
scenarios.modelAnswer: The arbitrage depends on scarcity, and CES is the opposite of a scarce-stunt market — mascots, costumes, and gimmicks are standard operating procedure, so a gorilla suit sits in a crowded market for the same reporter attention. Chargify's bull worked at SXSW in 2010 partly because SXSW then had fewer coordinated brand stunts than CES does today. The founder should check: (1) how many costumed mascots/stunts were on the floor at CES last year? If the answer is 'dozens,' the arbitrage is gone in this venue and format; (2) does the gorilla suit carry any payload — does it communicate what the product is? If it's just a gorilla, even successful coverage won't convert; (3) is there an empty-market format they could substitute — a stunt at a different venue, a different format entirely (e.g., taking out full-page ads in the CES daily newspaper the attendees actually read), or a format CES bans, which sometimes creates more coverage than the stunt itself. The lesson is: don't copy the *format* of a successful stunt; copy the *empty-market reasoning* that made it work.
rubric: Identifies CES as a crowded stunt market (arbitrage largely closed)
Notes the gorilla suit carries no product payload
Distinguishes copying format vs. copying empty-market reasoning
Suggests at least one alternative that genuinely exploits an empty market
Explains why context/timing (SXSW 2010 vs. CES 2026) matters for the arbitrage

scenarios.setup: A founder got great coverage for a clever launch stunt. Three months later he runs an almost-identical stunt expecting the same coverage. Almost no press covers it.
scenarios.question: What does the arbitrage frame predict here, and what's the right move for the next stunt?
scenarios.modelAnswer: The frame predicts exactly this. The first stunt's coverage came from scarcity — no one else was doing that thing. The second stunt is the same thing in the same empty market, except now it's no longer empty: the market's own prior coverage closed the arbitrage, because reporters don't cover the same trick twice. The right move for the next stunt is to find a *new* empty-market position: change the format, change the venue, or change the axis of surprise. What stays constant between stunts is the payload (what the product is about) and the brand voice; what must change is the specific scarcity being exploited. This is why the Traction authors warn that unconventional PR success is unpredictable — each stunt has to rediscover its own empty market.
rubric: Explains that the first stunt's own coverage closes that specific arbitrage
Articulates that reporters don't cover the same trick twice
Prescribes changing format/venue/axis while keeping payload and voice constant
Connects to the unpredictability takeaway in the chapter
Doesn't conclude 'stunts don't work' — correctly scopes the problem to format reuse

teachback.prompt: Teach the attention-arbitrage mechanism to a skeptical CFO who thinks stunts are 'unserious.' Cover: (1) what scarce resource is being arbitraged, (2) why the conventional market is crowded and the unconventional market isn't, (3) why the gap doesn't close (three structural frictions), and (4) what the 'payload inside the spectacle' principle implies for which stunts actually convert.
rubric: Names journalist/reader attention as the scarce good
Explains the crowded-vs-empty market framing explicitly
Gives all three structural frictions that keep the arbitrage open
Explains payload-inside-spectacle with at least one concrete example
Argument is delivered as financial logic (expected value, per-dollar return), suitable for a CFO

concepts.title: Who Unconventional PR Fits (and Who It Torches)

pretest.prompt: Two companies are deciding whether to invest in unconventional PR. Company A sells a consumer travel-booking app to adults 25–45. Company B sells a compliance workflow tool to risk officers at regulated banks. Before reading — for which one is unconventional PR more likely to work, and what are the two or three specific attributes of the business (not the tactic) that actually drive the answer?
pretest.modelAnswer: Company A is much more likely to get a positive return. The attributes that drive the answer are: (1) the buyer's tolerance for novelty and brand-voice stunts (consumer adults tolerate and often enjoy playful stunts; risk officers at regulated banks are actively trained to distrust anything that looks non-serious); (2) the breadth of the buying audience — consumer audiences are huge, so even modest spectacle-driven coverage reaches plausible buyers, while B's buyers number in the hundreds globally and are not reached through mass press; (3) the procurement path — A's users self-sign-up, so press-driven curiosity converts quickly, while B's sales require trust-building across legal, compliance, and security teams, for whom a stunt is a *negative* signal. Unconventional PR fits brand-adjacent, consumer-facing, novelty-tolerant companies; it actively damages regulated-B2B / high-trust-procurement companies.

lesson.markdown:
## The fit question is not optional

The attention-arbitrage mechanism says stunts can produce huge coverage per dollar. That is not the same as saying stunts produce *customers* per dollar for every business. The channel has a real fit profile, and for some companies the math actively runs backward — coverage you didn't want, from audiences you can't sell to, with brand damage among the audience you can.

## Where unconventional PR fits well

Three attributes, roughly in order of importance:

1. **The buyer tolerates (or rewards) novelty.** Consumer, prosumer, and brand-adjacent businesses live in markets where a memorable story is an asset. Virgin, Hipmunk, Half.com, Dollar Shave Club, Blendtec all sell to audiences who enjoy or forgive playful brand voice. A stunt that makes them laugh compounds affection.
2. **The buying audience is large and mass-addressable.** Stunts produce impressions at scale; impressions matter when a plausible customer is one in ten of the people who see the story. A consumer app with a TAM of tens of millions benefits from a 40-million-impression spike. A niche tool with 3,000 possible buyers globally barely notices it.
3. **The purchase is low-friction.** Press-driven curiosity converts best when the user can try the product in minutes. Sign up, install, free tier — stunts and click-through-to-signup have compatible time horizons. When conversion requires a months-long procurement cycle, the press moment fades before the deal closes.

Secondary positive signals: a visual or demonstrable product (Will It Blend? only works because you can watch the blender); a brand that already has some voice (not a blank generic identity); a team with creative-execution capacity in-house.

## Where unconventional PR torches budget

The same three attributes, inverted, produce the anti-fit:

1. **Regulated industries and high-trust procurement.** Healthcare, finance, defense, enterprise security, legal. Buyers in these markets are *negatively* selected for risk tolerance — their job is to not get burned. A stunt signals that you are not a serious vendor, which is a procurement-killing signal even if the stunt was genuinely clever. The ROI is not low; it's negative.
2. **Narrow, named buyers.** If your total addressable market is under a few thousand named accounts and each has a specific buyer, mass press is the wrong instrument. You don't need 40 million impressions; you need 500 correct meetings. Unconventional PR can still *support* named-account selling (the stunt gives your salespeople something to open a call with), but it can't lead.
3. **Long, multi-stakeholder purchase cycles.** When procurement involves security review, legal review, vendor onboarding, and multi-quarter pilots, the press moment has decayed before anyone signs. The coverage feeds neither the pipeline (wrong lead type) nor the close (decision-makers aren't press-driven).

## Ambiguous middle: prosumer and SMB

Many businesses sit in a middle band — prosumer tools, SMB SaaS, small-agency software. Here the answer is 'test carefully.' A small stunt ($1–5k, see next concept on testing) can reveal whether the audience converts from press-driven exposure without committing a full campaign's budget.

## The brand-voice gate

One more gate: does your brand voice *support* a stunt? A $5,000 'we're fun!' stunt grafted onto a sober, professional brand reads as desperate rather than playful. Before doing any unconventional PR, founders should be able to answer: 'If a customer saw this stunt and then visited our website, would it feel like the same company?' If no, either fix the brand first or stay out of the channel.

workedExample.setup: You're advising three founders who all saw the Grasshopper chocolate-grasshopper story and want to do something similar. (A) Ravi runs a $29/mo consumer habit tracker. (B) Lena runs a $50k/yr enterprise compliance platform for banks. (C) Marcus runs a $299/mo prosumer video-editing tool.
workedExample.walkthrough:
Apply the fit attributes to each.

**Ravi (consumer habit tracker).** Strong fit on all three. Consumers tolerate novelty, TAM is broad (tens of millions of adults setting habits), purchase is a two-click signup. A stunt like 'we'll mail you a free one-year physical habit journal, signed by the founder, to the first 500 people who install the app' sits inside his budget and drives signups directly. Unconventional PR is a plausible inner-ring channel for Ravi.

**Lena (enterprise compliance for banks).** Anti-fit on all three. Her buyers are compliance officers whose job is to be boring and cautious. A stunt signals unseriousness — her prospects will use it as a reason *not* to buy. Her TAM is a few hundred named banks; mass press can't find them and may alienate the ones that read it. Her sales cycle is 9–18 months with legal, security, and procurement gates. Recommendation: unconventional PR does not belong even in her Potential ring. Customer-appreciation gestures (a thoughtful onboarding concierge, handwritten thank-you notes to existing customers) are the one piece of this channel that safely applies, because they don't involve public spectacle.

**Marcus (prosumer video editing).** Ambiguous middle. Prosumers — freelancers, YouTubers, small agencies — tolerate novelty but make considered purchases. A small test makes sense: produce one video-editing challenge with a $2k prize, seed it to ten micro-influencers, measure signups over 30 days. Depending on the result, Marcus either invests further or deprioritizes. The right move for ambiguous-middle cases is not 'skip the channel' but 'buy the cheap experiment before committing.'

The general pattern: the fit answer is rarely 'yes to stunts, no to appreciation' or vice versa. Customer-appreciation gestures apply almost universally (including to Lena's compliance platform). It's the publicity-stunt half of the channel that has sharp fit constraints.

contrast.explanation: Chapter 6's product-fit concept for viral marketing asks whether the product's structural mechanics can sustain a viral loop. This concept asks whether the business's *audience and purchase context* will convert press-driven exposure. A product can have great viral fit and bad unconventional-PR fit (a free collaboration tool that spreads by invite inside companies but whose buyers ignore press), or vice versa (a consumer brand with no structural viral loop but big reach from a single stunt). Fit is channel-specific; don't infer one from the other.

flashcards.front: Name the three positive fit attributes for unconventional PR.
flashcards.back: (1) Buyer tolerates or rewards novelty. (2) Buying audience is large and mass-addressable. (3) Purchase is low-friction — signup, free tier, fast conversion. Consumer / prosumer / brand-adjacent businesses typically hit all three.

flashcards.front: Name three anti-fit conditions under which unconventional PR torches budget (or worse).
flashcards.back: (1) Regulated industries / high-trust procurement — stunts signal unseriousness and actively hurt sales. (2) Narrow, named-account TAM — mass press reaches the wrong audience. (3) Long, multi-stakeholder purchase cycles — the press moment decays before the deal closes.

flashcards.front: For regulated-industry B2B buyers (finance, healthcare, defense), unconventional PR often produces a ______ ROI, not merely a low one — the stunt is read as a signal of ______.
flashcards.back: negative ; unseriousness — risk-trained buyers use a stunt as a reason to disqualify you.

flashcards.front: Which half of unconventional PR can safely apply even to a regulated enterprise B2B company? (a) Publicity stunts. (b) Customer appreciation gestures. (c) Both. (d) Neither.
flashcards.back: (b). Handwritten notes, thank-you gifts, concierge onboarding, surprise credits — these compound goodwill without triggering the unseriousness signal. Publicity stunts, by contrast, actively damage trust with regulated buyers.

flashcards.front: What's the 'brand-voice gate' and why does it matter?
flashcards.back: The test: if a customer saw your stunt and then visited your website, would it feel like the same company? If not, the stunt reads as desperate rather than playful. Brand voice is a gate because a mismatched stunt damages the brand even when the stunt gets coverage — impressions without compatibility produce negative lift.

scenarios.setup: A founder of a $200k/yr cybersecurity platform selling to CISOs at Fortune 500 companies says: 'I want to do unconventional PR. I'm thinking a billboard in Silicon Valley that roasts our biggest competitor's recent breach.'
scenarios.question: Evaluate the fit and the specific idea. Recommend what to do instead if you disagree.
scenarios.modelAnswer: Both the channel fit and the specific idea are wrong for this business. Channel fit: cybersecurity sales to Fortune 500 CISOs is the archetypal anti-fit — regulated buyers, small named-account TAM (a few thousand companies globally), 9–18 month procurement cycles with security and legal gates. Stunts signal unseriousness to exactly the buyer profile who has to justify vendor choice to a board after a breach. Specific-idea problems: (a) roasting a competitor's breach weaponizes a tragedy their buyers also fear — it reads as schadenfreude, not confidence; (b) CISOs talk to each other and will remember the billboard against this vendor later; (c) the Silicon Valley location targets peers and press, not actual buyers who are at Fortune 500 HQs nationwide. Recommended substitutes: (1) publish serious, well-resourced original research on breach patterns — this is conventional PR + content, not unconventional, but it's the right channel for this audience; (2) invest the customer-appreciation half of unconventional PR — executive briefings, thoughtful gifts to CISO champions, post-sale concierge onboarding. These don't trigger the unseriousness signal and compound over long sales cycles.

Flags specific risks in the billboard idea (schadenfreude read, geographic mismatch, memory among CISOs)
Recommends keeping customer-appreciation gestures (safe half of the channel)

Does not dismiss unconventional PR in general — correctly scopes the 'not for you' to this specific business

scenarios.setup: A founder runs a consumer meditation app ($12/mo). She says: 'Unconventional PR obviously fits us — we're consumer, big TAM, low-friction signup. We should go all-in on stunts.'
scenarios.question: Is 'go all-in on stunts' the correct read of the fit? What nuances is she missing?
scenarios.modelAnswer: She's right that the fit attributes are positive, but 'go all-in' is the wrong conclusion for two reasons. First, the brand-voice gate matters especially for meditation: a loud, wacky stunt may clash with a calm, trustworthy brand promise, producing impressions at the cost of brand integrity. The stunt has to be *quiet-weird* — novel in a way consistent with the product (e.g., opening a free pop-up silence room in Times Square) rather than loud-weird (e.g., a stuntman in a costume). Second, Bullseye still applies: fit unlocks the channel as a candidate, not as a commitment. She should pick 1–2 cheap stunt tests alongside customer-appreciation gestures, measure conversion, and decide whether unconventional PR earns inner-ring status versus other candidates (viral loops, content/SEO, paid). 'Go all-in' skips the testing step and concentrates budget before there's evidence the specific stunts she'd run actually convert her specific audience.
rubric: Agrees fit is positive but catches the 'go all-in' overreach
Applies the brand-voice gate to meditation specifically
Distinguishes 'channel fits us' from 'this particular stunt will work'
Reconnects to the Bullseye testing discipline — fit unlocks testing, not commitment
Suggests the customer-appreciation side as a complementary lower-risk investment

pretest.prompt: Make a list, from memory, of five specific unconventional-PR plays you could run tomorrow with a $10k budget. For each, write one sentence on what's being arbitraged (what attention or goodwill pool the play draws from) and one on the biggest risk.
pretest.modelAnswer: Reasonable answers: (1) competitive stunt at a rival's event — arbitrages the rival's already-assembled audience; risk = legal/trademark blowback. (2) Branded viral video demonstrating the product in an extreme way — arbitrages YouTube/TikTok discovery; risk = tone clash and no conversion payload. (3) Contest with a notable prize aligned with target-user aspirations — arbitrages participants' social sharing; risk = mercenary entrants who don't convert. (4) Handwritten notes + small gifts to the first 500 customers — arbitrages rarity of physical mail; risk = scale limits and fulfillment cost. (5) Customer-referred-to-press program — arbitrages reporters' hunger for real user stories; risk = losing narrative control. Self-assessment: if your list came out mostly stunts with no appreciation plays, you're missing the compounding half of the channel.

lesson.markdown:
## The play menu

Unconventional PR is an unusual channel in that the 'tactics' list is genuinely a menu — most founders don't come to it with strong priors. Here is the working set.

### Stunts

**Competitive stunts.** Show up at a rival's event or venue with a message that reframes the rival's weakness as your strength. WePay's 600-pound block of ice at PayPal's conference. DuckDuckGo's billboard in Google's backyard. The arbitrage: the rival has already assembled your target audience; you pay a tiny fraction of their cost to get in front of it. Risk: legal/trademark, and a real possibility the audience reads you as punching down.

**Launch-moment stunts.** A large engineered event to create a national-press moment at launch. Half.com renaming Halfway, Oregon. Grasshopper's chocolate-covered grasshoppers to 5,000 influencers with a video about entrepreneurship. The arbitrage: launch moments are scarce windows where a story has an inherent hook; spend the entire stunt budget into that window rather than across the year.

**Viral videos.** Produced content designed to spread on YouTube, TikTok, or social feeds. Blendtec's 'Will It Blend?' series. Dollar Shave Club's launch video (12,000 customers in 48 hours, 8M-plus views over time). The arbitrage is platform discovery algorithms, not traditional press. The conversion payload needs to sit inside the content: Blendtec's videos literally demonstrate blender power, so watching *is* the product pitch.

### Customer appreciation (the compounding half)

**Physical gifts.** Hipmunk's luggage tags and handwritten notes to the first several hundred Twitter mentions. Grasshopper's Skittles, homemade cookies, and Starbucks gift cards. The arbitrage: physical mail is so rare now that any piece of it lands with outsized emotional weight for the cost. Scales to a few hundred per batch, not millions.

**Contests and giveaways.** Shopify's Build-a-Business competition ($3.5M+ in platform sales from 1,900 new customers). Dropbox's Dropquest (half a million participants in year one). Hipmunk's $500 Mother's Day flower giveaway, which produced coverage and long-term brand affection wildly disproportionate to spend. The arbitrage: contests borrow participants' social networks as distribution.

**Referred-customer press.** When reporters come to your company, route them to your customers instead of pitching your own product. Grasshopper does this. The customer gets unexpected press; the customer's loyalty multiplies; you get indirect coverage that reads as credible because a customer is telling it. The arbitrage: you trade a conventional 'company quote' slot (low conversion) for a 'customer story' slot (high conversion) the reporter actually wants.

**Customer-support-as-marketing.** Zappos's policy stance: customer support is a marketing investment, not a cost center. They don't track average-call-time because short calls aren't the goal — resolution quality is. Long calls in which a rep orders a pizza for a stranded customer or helps exchange workout clothes for a deep fryer (real Zappos stories) produce stories customers retell for years. The arbitrage: each call is a chance to create a word-of-mouth asset at marginal cost, not a cost to minimize.

**Customer dinners.** When leadership travels, invite local customers out. Small budget, large relational lift. Doesn't produce press but produces the underlying goodwill that stunts convert against.

## How stunts and appreciation interlock

The stunts produce spikes; the appreciation produces the baseline. Run only stunts and you get sawtooth growth — a high, then silence, then another high, then silence, with no organic word-of-mouth between events. Run only appreciation and you get steady word-of-mouth but no spikes big enough to move the company's trajectory. The template from Grasshopper: both, simultaneously, with dedicated owners for each.

## Where to start

The practical order for most founders: start with customer-appreciation (cheap, low-risk, builds the baseline, reveals which customers are likely evangelists). After two or three months of that, attempt a single stunt test sized for your budget (see the next concept). This order front-loads the lower-variance compounding work before betting on the higher-variance spike work.

workedExample.setup: Dana runs a 10-person prosumer accounting tool for freelancers ($19/mo). She has $15k for unconventional PR over the next quarter and a 2-person marketing team. She's never run anything in this channel. She asks for a concrete 90-day program that uses both halves.
workedExample.walkthrough:
A 90-day program covering both halves within the $15k envelope.

**Month 1 — baseline.** Build the appreciation engine first. Spend: ~$1.5k. Actions: (1) every new paying customer receives a handwritten card + a $5 coffee gift card within 72 hours of signup (budgeted for ~200 new customers); (2) identify the 50 most enthusiastic existing customers (NPS 9–10, multiple referrals, or vocal on Twitter) and send each a small branded gift (tax-themed pun socks, e.g., 'Deduct This') with a handwritten note from the founder. Owner: one marketing team member as standing responsibility.

**Month 2 — stunt preparation.** Spend: ~$1k on research, no execution yet. Actions: the team brainstorms 20 stunt ideas, filtered through three gates — (a) is this independently newsworthy to freelance/creative-economy press? (b) does it carry a payload about the product's actual differentiator (simplicity of freelance taxes)? (c) can we execute on $8k? The survivors: a 'freelancer tax-refund lottery' where Dana publicly pays one random freelancer's quarterly estimated taxes for a year ($3–5k prize); a pop-up 'tax amnesty booth' at a freelancer conference where attendees get their last-quarter receipts sorted live; a parody 'IRS form simplified' printable that goes viral on freelance Twitter.

**Month 3 — execute one stunt + continue appreciation.** Stunt budget: ~$8k. Dana picks the tax-refund lottery: $5k prize, $1k production and PR push, $1k user-generated content seeding, $1k reserve. The lottery is announced on a launch day, runs for three weeks of submissions, picks a winner on camera, and the winner's refund payment is publicly posted. Payload inside spectacle: the product's differentiator (freelance-tax simplicity) is explicit in every mention because the prize *is* taxes done.

**Remaining $3.5k reserve.** Holds budget for one follow-up customer-referred-press moment — when the press covers the lottery winner, Dana routes interview requests to the winner and the runners-up, not to herself.

**Why this shape.** The appreciation engine runs continuously because it's what compounds. The stunt is single, well-funded, and carefully payload-tested, because one good stunt outperforms four rushed ones. The ordering (appreciation first, stunt second) means that by the time the stunt runs, Dana has a small cohort of vocal early customers who will amplify it organically — the spike sits on top of a real baseline, not in a silent pond.

contrast.explanation: The definition concept tells you what makes something count as unconventional PR (creating news vs. pitching). This concept is the concrete menu of plays that instantiate the definition. Founders can recite the definition correctly and still freeze when asked 'so what do I actually do tomorrow.' The tactics menu closes that gap — and the interlock between stunts (spikes) and appreciation (baseline) is the piece most founders miss when they treat stunts as the entire channel.

flashcards.front: Name the three main stunt sub-types and one example of each.
flashcards.back: Competitive stunts (WePay's block of ice at PayPal's conference; DuckDuckGo's billboard in Google's backyard). Launch-moment stunts (Half.com renaming Halfway, Oregon; Grasshopper chocolate-grasshoppers). Viral videos (Blendtec's 'Will It Blend?'; Dollar Shave Club's launch video).

flashcards.front: Name at least four customer-appreciation tactics from the chapter.
flashcards.back: Physical gifts (Hipmunk's luggage tags, Grasshopper's cookies/gift cards), contests and giveaways (Shopify Build-a-Business, Dropquest, Hipmunk Mother's Day), referred-customer press (routing reporters to customers instead of pitching your own CEO), customer-support-as-marketing (Zappos long calls, untracked call-time), customer dinners when leadership travels.

flashcards.front: Stunts produce ______; appreciation produces ______. Running only stunts produces sawtooth growth; running only appreciation produces a baseline with no moments large enough to move the company's trajectory.
flashcards.back: spikes ; the baseline — the two interlock. Mature programs run both simultaneously with dedicated owners.

flashcards.front: Zappos deliberately does not track average call-time. Which principle of unconventional PR is that a concrete application of?
flashcards.back: Customer-support-as-marketing — framing each support interaction as a word-of-mouth asset rather than a cost to minimize. Short calls optimize cost; long calls optimize stories. The Zappos policies (free return shipping, unscripted support) flow from this reframing.

flashcards.front: What's the practical ordering the chapter recommends for a founder new to unconventional PR, and why?
flashcards.back: Start with customer-appreciation (cheap, low-risk, compounding, reveals evangelists), run it continuously for 2–3 months, then attempt a single well-funded stunt test. The ordering front-loads lower-variance work and means the first stunt lands on a real baseline of vocal customers rather than in a silent pond.

scenarios.setup: A founder says: 'Our unconventional PR program this year is four stunts, one per quarter. That's it. Appreciation is handled by support tickets.'
scenarios.question: What's wrong with this shape, using the stunts-vs-appreciation interlock?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Two things. First, four stunts in one year with no appreciation program produces classic sawtooth growth — a spike, three months of silence, another spike, three months of silence. The organic baseline between stunts is missing, so each stunt starts from near-zero amplification. Second, 'appreciation is handled by support tickets' conflates resolving problems with creating delight. Support ticket resolution avoids negative word-of-mouth; it does not produce positive word-of-mouth. Real appreciation programs (handwritten notes, small gifts, contests, referred-customer press, surprise upgrades) are a separate discipline with its own budget and owner. Fix: keep the four stunts but allocate a second, standing owner and roughly 25–40% of the unconventional-PR budget to a continuous appreciation program that runs between spikes. That way each stunt sits on top of a live baseline of vocal customers, and the company's growth curve stops looking like a sawtooth.
rubric: Identifies the sawtooth pattern as the failure mode
Distinguishes support-resolution from appreciation-as-delight
Prescribes a second, standing owner and a budget share for appreciation
Explains why the stunt's own amplification depends on the appreciation baseline
Keeps the stunts — doesn't over-correct to 'appreciation only'

scenarios.setup: A founder is running an annual Build-a-Business-style contest with a $25k prize. After three years, participation is flat and coverage has shrunk each year.
scenarios.question: What's going on, and what would you change?
scenarios.modelAnswer: This is the format-arbitrage-closing pattern applied to contests. The first year's contest was novel in the founder's market; reporters and participants covered it for the novelty. By year three, the contest itself is predictable — same format, same prize tier, same timing — so the scarcity it exploited is gone. Participation flattens because the audience has already signed up or moved on; coverage shrinks because reporters cover firsts and milestones, not recurrences. Fixes: (a) change the axis of surprise while keeping the brand and payload constant — e.g., same prize value but now delivered in an unexpected form (winner's business gets a cross-country publicity tour instead of cash); (b) raise the stakes meaningfully (a six-figure prize like Shopify runs); (c) rotate the contest theme annually so each year is a fresh news hook; (d) turn the contest into a story program where winners become customer-press assets year-round, not just on announcement day. What *not* to do: run the same contest for a fourth year expecting different results. The contest itself is fine; its scarcity has just been consumed.
rubric: Identifies format-arbitrage closing as the underlying mechanism
Names at least two distinct fixes (axis of surprise, stakes, theme rotation, year-round story program)
Correctly rules out 'run the same contest again harder' as a solution
Keeps the contest format alive rather than killing the whole play
Connects this to the broader principle that arbitrages close within specific formats
