pretest.prompt: A founder tells you: 'You can't really test unconventional PR — it's inherently a big swing. Either you commit to a $50k stunt or you don't do the channel.' Before reading on — is this right? If not, what would a cheap unconventional-PR test actually look like, and what is it measuring?
pretest.modelAnswer: It's not right. The big-or-nothing frame confuses the ceiling of a single stunt with the cost of testing the channel. A cheap test is a scaled-down version of a candidate stunt (or a tiny appreciation program) sized so that if it flops you've learned something specific for one-digit-percent of a full campaign. What you're measuring: (1) press-worthiness — does anyone external pick up or share the thing unprompted? (2) audience reaction — do the responses look like your ICP? (3) conversion — do signups or inbound interest actually move? A $500 version of a contest, 50 handwritten notes to targeted customers, a single unboosted viral-video attempt, a lightweight localized stunt — all are cheap probes that validate the elements of the eventual full-scale play.

lesson.markdown:
## Why testing is harder here than in other channels

Most traction channels have obvious cheap-test templates. Paid ads have $50 campaigns; SEO has single-page experiments; sales has a batch of 30 outbound emails. Unconventional PR resists that pattern because the stunts that worked (Half.com's town, DuckDuckGo's billboard) were one-off commitments with no natural smaller version. Founders therefore often skip testing and either jump in with a full-budget stunt or skip the channel entirely — both avoidable errors.

The reframe: what you're testing in unconventional PR is not 'will this exact stunt succeed' but **the elements that determine whether stunts in this shape succeed for this business**. Those elements have cheap tests.

## The five cheap-test patterns

### 1. The press-worthiness self-test
Before spending anything, answer a blunt question in writing: *if I read about this stunt on a competitor's blog, would I forward it to someone?* If no, the stunt isn't press-worthy and no budget will fix that. This test costs zero and disqualifies maybe half of the stunt ideas a team generates.

A sharper version: write the headline the stunt would generate, verbatim, before executing. If the imagined headline is bland ('Company X Hosts Giveaway'), the actual headline will be too. If it's specific and surprising ('Startup Pays a Small Oregon Town $70k to Rename Itself'), the underlying stunt has a chance.

### 2. Tiny versions of the full stunt
Run a scaled-down version to 100 people, one city, or one channel. Hipmunk tested customer-appreciation gifts on 'the first several hundred' Twitter mentioners before scaling the program. You are not trying to capture the full upside; you are asking whether the mechanism fires at all.

For stunts specifically, a tiny version often means a local or narrow-audience execution — a stunt at one conference, a giveaway in one city, a video seeded to one subreddit — before committing to a national version. If it doesn't move the needle at small scale, it's unlikely to move it at large scale; the dynamic is usually not 'go bigger and it will break through.'

### 3. Single-execution appreciation probes
Send 50 handwritten notes. Pick 20 customers at random and surprise each with a $10 coffee gift card. Watch what happens: do they mention it publicly? Do they refer anyone? Do they respond at all? At $500 total you learn whether your customer base is the kind that amplifies appreciation, which is the load-bearing question for scaling the appreciation half of the channel.

### 4. Cheap video tests before expensive video production
Dollar Shave Club's $5,000 video is the cautionary *upper* bound — most founders cannot replicate that budget or its comedic taste. The cheap test is to produce a 60-second rough video for $200–500, post it to one platform, and look at shares and completion rate in 72 hours. A video that can't move a small organic audience will not move a big one after you spend $20k polishing it.

### 5. Piggyback tests on existing events
Instead of engineering your own event, insert a small stunt into an event that already has an audience — a conference, a product launch window (yours or a competitor's), a cultural moment. Chargify's green bull at SXSW for $3k is the template: SXSW assembled the audience; Chargify paid only for the bull. This reduces the 'will anyone show up' risk, leaving only the 'does the stunt work on the audience present' question to test.

## What passes and what fails

A test passes when one or more of: (a) the signal is visibly positive at small scale, (b) you learned a specific, named thing about why the format fit or didn't, or (c) you now have clear hypotheses for the next, slightly larger version. A test fails silently when you spent $500 and learned nothing — usually because you didn't define up front what the probe was supposed to demonstrate. Before any unconventional-PR test, write the single question it's answering. Without that, the test is just a small version of the same leap of faith.

## Budget the loss, not the upside

One more discipline: size tests by the amount you can afford to lose, not the upside you're hoping for. A good stunt test costs 1–5% of the full-scale campaign budget and asks a specific question. If the test fails, you've lost 1–5% of the budget and disqualified one format. If it succeeds, you invest the remaining 95–99% with much more evidence. This is the same budgeting logic that makes Bullseye's Step 4 cheap parallel tests work — applied to a channel that is culturally resistant to being tested.

workedExample.setup: Jasmine runs a consumer pet-nutrition subscription ($40/mo for custom-formulated dog food). She has $3k to test whether unconventional PR is an inner-ring candidate before committing to a full campaign. Her team has three stunt ideas: (A) a 'worst dog food ingredient' investigation video, (B) a contest giving one subscriber a free year and a personalized dog portrait, (C) a physical pop-up 'dog taste-testing station' in a park.
workedExample.walkthrough:
Design cheap tests for each idea within the $3k budget.

**Pre-test: the headline test (zero cost).** Jasmine writes the headline each stunt would produce. (A) 'Pet-Food Startup Publishes Investigation of Competitor Ingredients' — specific, but skews combative; might work, might alienate. (B) 'Dog-Food Startup Gives Away Free Year + Custom Portrait' — bland; fails the headline test. (C) 'Dogs Taste-Test New Food in Brooklyn Park, Owners Get Samples' — charming, specific, visual. C and A survive; B is disqualified before spending anything.

**Test A: video probe ($500).** Produce a 90-second rough version of the investigation video using existing footage and a voiceover. Post to one dog-owner subreddit. Measure 72-hour shares, upvotes, and comment tone. What this test is answering: does the tone land with the target audience, or does it read as attack marketing? Budget: $500 for script, voiceover, and a small seeding push.

**Test C: piggyback tiny stunt ($1,500).** Run the pop-up at one single Saturday farmers' market in a specific neighborhood. Budget: $400 for a table and signage, $600 for sample inventory, $400 for a videographer to capture content, $100 miscellaneous. What this test is answering: (a) do dogs and owners engage (the easy yes), (b) does the capture produce organic content worth seeding, and (c) do any of the 200 attendees actually sign up within 48 hours using the tracked discount code.

**Reserve ($1,000).** Held for a follow-up on whichever test produces a positive signal. If the video lands, boost it and produce a longer version. If the pop-up lands, run it in three more cities the following month.

**Expected outcomes.** Jasmine is not expecting any single test to be 'the stunt.' She is testing the elements: does her audience amplify combative content (A); does physical-pop-up content convert organically to signups (C). After the two tests plus the disqualified B, she has a data-backed decision about which full-scale stunt to fund (if any), and she has spent $2k of the $3k doing it. Compare with the alternative of spending the whole $3k on a single un-tested $3k stunt — which would have taught her nothing generalizable if it flopped.

contrast.explanation: Chapter 4's inner-circle-tests concept teaches cheap traction tests as a general discipline across all channels. This concept is the channel-specific application to unconventional PR — which is unusual because most other channels have obvious test templates and unconventional PR doesn't. The channel-level insight is that tests in this channel probe *elements* (headline strength, tone fit, audience amplification, conversion payload) rather than whole stunts, because whole stunts don't have natural small versions. If you try to apply Chapter 4's advice literally without this channel-specific framing, you'll either conclude stunts can't be tested or run tests that cost too much to actually be cheap.

flashcards.front: What's the 'headline test' for a proposed stunt, and what does it cost?
flashcards.back: Write the headline the stunt would generate, verbatim, before executing. If the imagined headline is bland ('Company X Hosts Giveaway'), the real one will be too; disqualify. If it's specific and surprising, the stunt has a chance. Cost: zero. It disqualifies maybe half of brainstormed stunt ideas before any spend.

flashcards.front: A good unconventional-PR test costs ______% of the full-campaign budget and answers a single, pre-declared question.
flashcards.back: 1–5% — size tests by the amount you can afford to lose, not the upside you're hoping for. If it fails, you've lost a small fraction of budget and disqualified a format. If it succeeds, the remaining budget is deployed with much more evidence.

flashcards.front: Name three of the five cheap-test patterns for unconventional PR.
flashcards.back: Any three of: (1) press-worthiness / headline self-test, (2) tiny version of the full stunt (local, narrow audience), (3) single-execution appreciation probe (50 notes, $10 gift cards), (4) rough $200–500 video test before expensive production, (5) piggyback on existing events rather than engineering your own.

flashcards.front: Why does unconventional PR resist the obvious cheap-test template that other channels have?
flashcards.back: Because successful stunts (Half.com's town rename, DuckDuckGo's billboard) were one-off commitments with no natural smaller version. The fix is a reframe: you're not testing 'will this exact stunt succeed' — you're testing the *elements* (headline strength, tone fit, amplification, payload conversion) that determine whether stunts in this shape work for this business.

flashcards.front: A test 'fails silently' when…
flashcards.back: …you spent money and learned nothing generalizable — usually because you didn't define up front what the probe was supposed to demonstrate. Silent failure is the real enemy of cheap testing: the spend isn't the loss, the unclaimed learning is.

scenarios.setup: A founder says: 'We have $40k earmarked for a launch stunt. We want to go big and spend it all on one shot — that's how Half.com and DuckDuckGo did it.'
scenarios.question: What's wrong with this framing, and what's a better allocation?
scenarios.modelAnswer: The framing confuses the visible examples (single big stunts that worked) with the success base rate (unknown, but certainly not 100%). Half.com and DuckDuckGo are survivorship-biased — the flops don't get written up. The right allocation treats $40k as a campaign budget with a testing phase: spend $1–2k on 1–2 cheap tests (headline test is free; a tiny version of the stunt or a piggyback at an existing event might be $1k each), use the results to pick the format most likely to work, then spend the remaining $38k on that format with much more conviction. If both tests fail, reallocate the budget entirely — don't force a stunt just because the budget exists. The founder should also explicitly size the loss: 'if this test fails, I've lost 3% of the budget and learned which format doesn't fit my audience,' rather than 'if the big stunt fails, I've lost the whole budget and learned nothing specific.'
rubric: Flags survivorship bias in citing Half.com / DuckDuckGo as templates
Prescribes a 1–5% testing allocation with a specific budget split
Allows for the test-fails-both outcome (reallocate rather than force)
Frames the learning payoff, not just the financial loss
Does not argue against the big stunt itself, only against the pre-commitment without testing

scenarios.setup: A founder ran a $500 appreciation test — handwritten notes plus $10 gift cards to 50 random customers. Nobody mentioned it publicly. She concludes 'appreciation doesn't work for our audience' and moves on.
scenarios.question: Is her conclusion sound? What's the most likely flaw in her test design?
scenarios.modelAnswer: The conclusion is probably premature, and the most likely flaw is under-specification of what 'worked' means. 'Nobody mentioned it publicly' is one possible signal but not the only one — did recipients respond privately? Did their retention or referral rates in the subsequent 30 days differ from a control group? Did repeat purchase rates move? Appreciation often produces private loyalty that shows up in retention and referrals rather than public mentions, especially for non-broadcasting audiences. The test also didn't define up front what 'passes' meant, which is the silent-failure pattern: she spent $500 and can't answer whether the program works because she didn't specify the measurement. Better redesign: define two or three measurable signals (public mention, reply rate, 30-day retention vs. matched control, referral rate) and the threshold for each before running the test. She should also consider whether 50 recipients is enough to detect anything — small absolute numbers mean even real effects can look like noise.
rubric: Identifies the premature conclusion
Names public mentions as only one of several possible signals
Suggests private/behavioral signals (retention, referrals, reply rate)
Flags the absence of pre-declared success criteria (silent-failure pattern)
Notes sample-size limitations for small probes

teachback.prompt: Teach a founder how to test unconventional PR cheaply before committing a full campaign. Cover: (1) why this channel resists the obvious cheap-test pattern, (2) the reframe (test elements, not whole stunts), (3) at least three of the five cheap-test patterns, and (4) the silent-failure trap and how to avoid it.
rubric: Explains why whole stunts don't have natural small versions
Articulates the element-testing reframe clearly
Lists at least three cheap-test patterns with examples
Explains silent failure and the fix (pre-declared single question and success criterion)
Sizes tests by loss tolerance, not upside hope

concepts.title: Pitfalls: Brand Damage, One-Hit-Wonders, and Misread Audiences

pretest.prompt: Before reading — list the three most dangerous ways an unconventional-PR effort can fail, beyond simply 'not getting coverage.' For each, name the specific warning sign a founder should notice early.
pretest.modelAnswer: Three serious failure modes: (1) Brand damage — the stunt gets coverage, but the coverage's tone or framing is hostile, sarcastic, or mocking. Warning sign: your own customers are among the people sharing the coverage with raised eyebrows, not laughter. (2) One-hit-wonder decay — a successful stunt produces a signup spike that does not seed a baseline. Warning sign: 30 days post-stunt your acquisition curve is lower than it was the week before the stunt. (3) Misread audience — coverage lands in publications whose readers aren't your buyers (a tech stunt covered by tech blogs when your actual customers are small-business owners). Warning sign: the conversion rate from press to signup is an order of magnitude below your usual paid or organic rates, because the coverage reached the wrong people. Less severe but common: legal/trademark blowback on competitive stunts; copying a format whose arbitrage has already closed.

lesson.markdown:
## Why pitfalls get their own concept

Unconventional PR's upside stories (Half.com, Dollar Shave Club, Blendtec) are widely told. The failures are mostly silent — unsuccessful stunts don't get written up, so founders approach the channel with a systematically optimistic picture. Knowing the failure modes in advance is worth roughly as much as knowing the tactics, because the downside of a bad stunt is often greater than simply not running one.

## The three serious pitfalls

### 1. Brand damage

A stunt can get the coverage and simultaneously damage the brand the coverage was supposed to build. This happens when the tone of the stunt clashes with the product's promise (a wacky stunt from a brand that sells seriousness), when the stunt punches down at sympathetic targets (mocking users, customers, or vulnerable groups), or when the stunt touches a sensitive topic the team underestimated (anything near politics, tragedy, identity, or active controversy).

Warning signs: your own customers share the coverage with raised eyebrows, not laughter; the comments on any coverage skew toward 'yikes' or 'not a good look'; employees are visibly uncomfortable defending it.

The fix is pre-launch: run the stunt by three to five people outside your marketing team — a customer, a junior employee, someone in legal — and ask a specific question: 'what's the worst-case reading of this?' If more than one person volunteers the same worst-case read, the stunt is too risky as designed.

### 2. The one-hit-wonder problem

A stunt succeeds, produces a large acquisition spike, and then… nothing. Thirty days later, acquisition is at or below pre-stunt levels. This is the classic pattern when unconventional PR is run without the appreciation baseline, and without a product or onboarding ready to retain the spike.

Two distinct mechanisms cause it. First, the signups from press coverage are lower-intent on average than signups from need-driven search or referral — many churn within weeks. Second, the spike burns whatever latent demand the press reached, with nothing seeding a follow-on wave.

The fix has two halves. Product side: make sure the onboarding for press-driven signups handles their intent curve (they came because a stunt caught their attention, not because they urgently needed your product — design an activation flow that earns attention rather than assuming it). Channel side: run appreciation and secondary stunts continuously, so the stunt is a peak inside a series rather than an isolated event.

### 3. Misread audiences

Press coverage is not generic. A stunt can get New York Times coverage that reaches a liberal-arts readership when your ICP is enterprise IT buyers — impressions happen, conversion doesn't. Or: coverage hits niche tech publications when your customers are small restaurant owners who read Yelp and local papers.

The warning sign is quantitative: the conversion rate from press exposure to signup is dramatically below your other channels' conversion rates, or qualitative: post-stunt customer interviews reveal the new signups don't match your ICP.

The fix is pre-launch audience mapping. Before committing to a stunt, list the publications most likely to cover it. Do your actual buyers read those publications? If no, the stunt may still happen (impressions have some residual value), but it shouldn't be counted on for conversion.

## Two lesser but common pitfalls

**Legal and trademark blowback** on competitive stunts. WePay's ice at PayPal's conference worked partly because it was clever enough not to cross legal lines. Many similar attempts do cross those lines and produce cease-and-desist news cycles that overshadow the original stunt.

**Format fatigue.** Copying the specific format of a famous stunt (another launch video, another mascot costume, another town rename) after its arbitrage has closed. The format worked once because it was scarce; by the time you copy it, it isn't. The durable move is to copy the *reasoning* (find an empty market, encode the payload in the spectacle) not the format.

## The unpredictability honest-fact

The authors are explicit that even well-planned stunts frequently flop. David Hauser's team at Grasshopper ran failed March Madness promotions, dead ticket giveaways, and abandoned dancing-grasshopper videos. This is not a sign the team was bad; it's a structural property of the channel. The right response is not to avoid the channel but to budget for flops and measure outcomes honestly — a 1-in-4 or 1-in-5 hit rate is reasonable, and a team that claims every stunt worked is almost certainly not attempting things risky enough to matter.

workedExample.setup: A mid-stage fintech startup ran a 'bank-rage' stunt — a video compilation of customers' frustrated voicemails to traditional banks, edited to music, posted publicly with customers' permission. The coverage was large (TechCrunch, The Verge, Bloomberg). Thirty days later, however: (a) three customers have withdrawn permission and one has threatened to sue, (b) the signup spike decayed completely and acquisition is back to baseline, (c) a notable share of comments on the coverage used words like 'tone-deaf' and 'punching down on customer service workers.' The founder wants to know which failure modes are in play and whether to run a follow-up.
workedExample.walkthrough:
Walk the three pitfalls against the observed signals.

**Brand damage is in play.** The 'tone-deaf' comments and the customer lawsuit threat are the warning signs: the stunt's tone landed differently from how the team predicted. The likely root cause: the team conceived it as 'bank-rage' (punching up at banks) but the video's execution made customer-service workers the implicit target. The outside-reviewer test probably wasn't run, because any customer service worker shown the draft would have flagged this. Follow-up response: issue a short, non-defensive public note acknowledging the workers-as-target reading and pull the video. Do not 'double down' with a defense — the coverage already exists and will not be re-opened in the founder's favor.

**One-hit-wonder is in play.** Acquisition back to baseline after 30 days is the textbook pattern. Part of this is product-side — press-driven signups are lower-intent, and if onboarding didn't re-engage them, they churned. Part is channel-side — no appreciation program or secondary activity kept the signal warm. Follow-up response: build the baseline. Customer-appreciation program to existing users starts this week; plan a second, lower-variance stunt in Q2; evaluate onboarding conversion specifically for press-driven signups vs. organic.

**Misread audience is partially in play.** TechCrunch/Verge/Bloomberg readers are a mixed audience; some are the fintech's target customers (tech-forward professionals) but many are not. Without channel-tagged tracking it's hard to know what fraction of the spike's signups were ICP-matched. Follow-up response: interview 20 of the signups to see whether they match ICP; use the answer to decide whether future stunts should aim for different publications.

**On running a follow-up.** Not yet. The brand-damage pitfall needs to be closed before another spend, and the team needs a retrospective on the tone mechanism that failed. Running a follow-up stunt into an active brand-damage cycle amplifies the damage. Do two months of customer-appreciation work and internal review, then revisit the stunt question in Q2.

contrast.explanation: The attention-arbitrage concept explains why stunts can produce disproportionate upside per dollar. This concept is the symmetric downside story: when the mechanism misfires, the damage can also be disproportionate. The two concepts together set the expected-value framing correctly — stunts are high-variance bets whose upside is real (arbitrage) but whose downside is also real (brand damage, one-hit-wonders, misread audiences). Founders who internalize the arbitrage without internalizing the pitfalls make reckless stunts; founders who internalize the pitfalls without the arbitrage skip the channel entirely.

flashcards.front: Name the three most serious unconventional-PR pitfalls and the warning sign for each.
flashcards.back: (1) Brand damage — warning sign: customers share coverage with raised eyebrows, comments skew 'yikes,' employees uncomfortable defending. (2) One-hit-wonder — warning sign: 30 days post-stunt, acquisition is at or below pre-stunt baseline. (3) Misread audience — warning sign: press-to-signup conversion an order of magnitude below other channels, or post-stunt customer interviews reveal signups don't match ICP.

flashcards.front: The 'worst-case reading' test: run the stunt by ______ people outside your marketing team and ask each for the worst-case interpretation. If ______ or more give the same worst-case read, the stunt is too risky as designed.
flashcards.back: 3–5 ; 2 (more than one) — this is the cheapest pre-launch brand-damage check.

flashcards.front: What are the two mechanisms behind the one-hit-wonder problem?
flashcards.back: (1) Press-driven signups are lower-intent on average and churn faster — if onboarding doesn't engage them, the spike decays via attrition. (2) The spike burns through whatever latent demand the press reached, with nothing seeding a follow-on wave. Fix is two-sided: press-specific onboarding + continuous appreciation baseline + planned secondary stunts.

flashcards.front: A founder's launch video becomes a meme and drives 50,000 signups. Two months later, DAU is below pre-launch levels. Which pitfall pattern is this?
flashcards.back: One-hit-wonder. The spike is real; the baseline didn't exist to catch the decay. The fix isn't 'don't do stunts' — it's to run appreciation continuously and design onboarding specifically for the intent curve of press-driven signups (they came for a stunt, not a product need).

flashcards.front: Why should you copy the reasoning of a successful stunt rather than its format?
flashcards.back: Because the format's success was a function of scarcity in that specific market at that specific time. Copying the format after the arbitrage has closed produces format fatigue — same mechanic, no scarcity, shrinking returns. Copying the reasoning (find an empty market, encode payload in the spectacle, match audience to publication) transfers to new formats.

scenarios.setup: A B2B SaaS startup's CMO proposes a stunt that mocks a class of users ('software developers who still use spreadsheets for project management — here's what we think of them,' with a cartoon). The CMO argues 'it's edgy, and edge gets coverage.'
scenarios.question: Which pitfall is most likely in play here, and how would you test it before committing?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Brand damage is the most likely pitfall. Mocking a class of users — especially one that overlaps with your potential buyers — is textbook punching-down, and the coverage that follows will often have a 'yikes' tone that clings to the brand long after the stunt fades. The cheap pre-launch test is the outside-reviewer exercise: show the concept (including the cartoon) to five people outside the marketing team, specifically including at least one person who fits the mocked profile (a developer who has used spreadsheets for project management), and ask each for the worst-case reading. If two or more volunteer 'this is insulting to developers' unprompted, the stunt is too risky as designed. A less risky rework: keep the edge but target the tool, not the user — 'spreadsheets were never meant to run a sprint' with a demonstration of why, framed as sympathetic to the user stuck using them. This preserves the 'edge gets coverage' logic while removing the punching-down vector.
rubric: Identifies brand damage / punching-down as the specific pitfall
Prescribes the outside-reviewer worst-case test
Specifies including a reviewer from the mocked profile
Offers a concrete rework that keeps the edge but changes the target
Addresses the CMO's 'edge gets coverage' argument without dismissing it

scenarios.setup: A team just ran a successful stunt that got coverage in three national tech publications. Leadership wants to double down: run the same format again quarterly.
scenarios.question: What's the concern, and what's a better follow-up strategy?
scenarios.modelAnswer: The concern is format fatigue — the arbitrage that made the first run successful has now been partially closed by the first run's own coverage. Running the same format quarterly will show diminishing returns per quarter: participation flatter, coverage thinner, audience familiarity climbing. A better follow-up strategy: (a) keep the brand and payload constant, (b) rotate the format axis of surprise each quarter (different venue, different medium, different twist), (c) invest at least 30% of the follow-up budget into customer-appreciation to build the baseline that the original stunt lacked, (d) measure each quarter's incremental coverage and signups independently so you catch the diminishing-returns signal before the fourth run. The goal is to treat the successful format as a proof-of-concept, not a template — what you learned is that your team can execute stunts well, not that this specific format is repeatable.
rubric: Names format fatigue / arbitrage closure as the concern
Distinguishes keeping brand/payload constant from rotating format
Allocates meaningful follow-up budget to the appreciation baseline
Prescribes independent per-quarter measurement to detect decay
Reframes the success as proof-of-execution-capability, not format repeatability

teachback.prompt: Teach an experienced founder the pitfalls of unconventional PR before their first stunt. Cover: (1) why the pitfalls matter as much as the tactics, (2) the three serious pitfalls with a warning sign for each, (3) the 'worst-case reading' pre-launch test, and (4) what a healthy stunt flop rate looks like and why aiming for zero flops is the wrong goal.
rubric: Explains why failures are systematically underreported (survivorship bias in visible stunts)
Lists all three serious pitfalls with warning signs
Describes the worst-case-reading test with specifics (3–5 reviewers, outside marketing)
Normalizes a 1-in-4 / 1-in-5 hit rate and explains why attempting only safe stunts is itself a failure
Argument is delivered with concrete examples, not abstract warnings
